title: How Cities Grew
summary: A narrator explains how cities developed from small settlements into large urban centres, driven by trade, industry, and the movement of people.
body: For most of human history, people lived in small villages and relied on farming to survive. Towns only began to appear when farming produced enough food to feed people who did not work in the fields. Craftsmen, merchants, and officials could then live in one place and trade with the surrounding countryside. These early towns were often built near rivers, which provided fresh water, a way to travel, and a means of transporting goods. Some towns became important trading posts and gradually attracted more people, growing into cities over many centuries. The rise of cities was not always a smooth process. Disease spread easily in crowded conditions, and many early cities lost large parts of their population to plague and other illnesses. Poor sanitation was a constant problem. Waste from homes and workshops flowed into the same rivers that people drank from, and this contaminated water caused widespread sickness. Despite these dangers, cities continued to attract migrants because they offered something that villages could not: the chance to earn a living in new ways and to be part of a larger community. The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late eighteenth century, transformed cities on a scale that had never been seen before. Factories replaced workshops, and machines did the work that had once required many skilled hands. Thousands of people moved from the countryside to cities in search of factory jobs, and the populations of industrial cities grew with remarkable speed. Manchester, for example, had around twenty-five thousand inhabitants in 1772 but nearly three hundred thousand by 1850. This rapid growth created serious problems. Housing was often overcrowded and poorly built. Workers lived close to factories, breathing air thick with smoke and dust. Children worked long hours in dangerous conditions. Social reformers, journalists, and eventually governments began to respond, passing laws that regulated working hours, improved housing standards, and required clean water supplies. By the early twentieth century, cities had become more liveable, although the gap between rich and poor neighbourhoods remained wide. Today more than half of the world's population lives in cities, and that proportion is expected to grow. Understanding how cities developed — their strengths and their failures — helps us think more carefully about how to build the cities of the future.

annotations.exampleEn: For centuries, most families relied on farming for their food.
Skilled craftsmen made tools, furniture, and clothing by hand.

annotations.meaningVi: trạm giao thương, điểm buôn bán
annotations.exampleEn: The fort grew into a city after becoming an important trading post.

annotations.meaningVi: vệ sinh công cộng, hệ thống xử lý rác thải
annotations.exampleEn: Improved sanitation reduced the spread of disease in cities.
Contaminated water from the river caused a cholera outbreak.
The contaminated supply caused widespread sickness across the city.

annotations.meaningVi: người di cư, người chuyển đến nơi mới
annotations.exampleEn: Migrants from rural areas flooded into the industrial cities.
The Industrial Revolution changed how goods were produced and how people worked.

annotations.phrase: on a scale that had never been seen before
annotations.meaningVi: ở quy mô chưa từng thấy trước đây
annotations.exampleEn: The flood destroyed the region on a scale that had never been seen before.
Many people left their villages in search of better opportunities.
The city grew with remarkable speed after the railway arrived.
The city has more than two million inhabitants today.
The overcrowded housing blocks had no proper ventilation.

annotations.meaningVi: những người cải cách xã hội
annotations.exampleEn: Social reformers campaigned for better working conditions in the factories.

annotations.meaningVi: điều chỉnh, kiểm soát bằng quy định
annotations.exampleEn: The government regulated the number of hours workers could be asked to work.

annotations.meaningVi: đáng sống, có điều kiện sống tốt
annotations.exampleEn: Green spaces and clean air make a city more liveable.

grammarNotes.title: Past Perfect — Describing what had happened before another past event
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài sử dụng thì quá khứ hoàn thành để diễn đạt các sự kiện xảy ra trước một thời điểm hoặc sự kiện khác trong quá khứ: 'machines did the work that had once required many skilled hands', 'on a scale that had never been seen before'. Cấu trúc: had + past participle. Thì này thường dùng để thiết lập bối cảnh hoặc tương phản với thì quá khứ đơn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses the past perfect to describe events that happened before another past point: 'machines did the work that had once required many skilled hands', 'on a scale that had never been seen before'. Form: had + past participle. This tense is used to establish context or contrast with the simple past.

grammarNotes.title: Non-defining Relative Clauses — Adding extra information with commas
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài có nhiều mệnh đề quan hệ không xác định, được đặt giữa hai dấu phẩy: 'The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late eighteenth century, transformed cities…'. Mệnh đề này cung cấp thông tin bổ sung (không cần thiết để xác định danh từ). So sánh với mệnh đề quan hệ xác định: 'towns that produced enough food' (không dấu phẩy). 'Which' được dùng cho vật, 'who' cho người trong mệnh đề không xác định.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses non-defining relative clauses set off by commas: 'The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late eighteenth century, transformed cities…'. These clauses give extra information not needed to identify the noun. Compare with defining clauses: 'towns that produced enough food' (no commas). 'Which' is used for things; 'who' for people in non-defining clauses.

translationVi:
Trong hầu hết lịch sử nhân loại, mọi người sống trong các ngôi làng nhỏ và phụ thuộc vào nông nghiệp để sinh tồn. Các thị trấn chỉ bắt đầu xuất hiện khi nông nghiệp sản xuất đủ lương thực để nuôi những người không làm việc ngoài đồng. Thợ thủ công, thương nhân và quan chức khi đó có thể sống tại một nơi và giao thương với vùng nông thôn xung quanh. Những thị trấn ban đầu này thường được xây dựng gần sông, nơi cung cấp nước ngọt, đường đi lại và phương tiện vận chuyển hàng hóa. Một số thị trấn trở thành các trạm thương mại quan trọng và dần dần thu hút nhiều người hơn, phát triển thành thành phố qua nhiều thế kỷ.

Sự phát triển của các thành phố không phải lúc nào cũng suôn sẻ. Bệnh tật lan truyền dễ dàng trong điều kiện đông đúc, và nhiều thành phố ban đầu mất đi phần lớn dân số vì dịch hạch và các bệnh khác. Vệ sinh kém là vấn đề thường trực. Chất thải từ nhà cửa và xưởng thợ chảy vào cùng những con sông mà người dân lấy nước uống, và nước ô nhiễm này gây ra bệnh tật trên diện rộng. Bất chấp những nguy hiểm này, các thành phố vẫn tiếp tục thu hút người di cư vì chúng cung cấp thứ mà làng quê không có: cơ hội kiếm sống theo những cách mới và trở thành một phần của cộng đồng lớn hơn.

Cuộc Cách mạng Công nghiệp, bắt đầu ở Anh vào cuối thế kỷ mười tám, đã biến đổi các thành phố theo quy mô chưa từng thấy trước đây. Các nhà máy thay thế xưởng thợ, và máy móc thực hiện công việc mà trước đây cần đến nhiều bàn tay lành nghề. Hàng nghìn người chuyển từ nông thôn lên thành phố để tìm kiếm việc làm trong nhà máy, và dân số của các thành phố công nghiệp tăng lên với tốc độ đáng kinh ngạc. Manchester, ví dụ, có khoảng hai mươi lăm nghìn cư dân vào năm 1772 nhưng gần ba trăm nghìn vào năm 1850.

Sự tăng trưởng nhanh chóng này tạo ra những vấn đề nghiêm trọng. Nhà ở thường chật chội và xây dựng kém chất lượng. Công nhân sống gần nhà máy, hít thở không khí đặc khói và bụi. Trẻ em làm việc nhiều giờ trong điều kiện nguy hiểm. Các nhà cải cách xã hội, nhà báo và cuối cùng là chính phủ bắt đầu phản ứng, ban hành các luật quy định giờ làm việc, cải thiện tiêu chuẩn nhà ở và yêu cầu cung cấp nước sạch. Đến đầu thế kỷ hai mươi, các thành phố đã trở nên dễ sống hơn, mặc dù khoảng cách giữa khu phố giàu và nghèo vẫn còn rộng.

Ngày nay hơn một nửa dân số thế giới sống trong các thành phố, và tỷ lệ đó dự kiến sẽ tiếp tục tăng. Hiểu được cách các thành phố phát triển — điểm mạnh và thất bại của chúng — giúp chúng ta suy nghĩ cẩn thận hơn về cách xây dựng các thành phố của tương lai.

questions.prompt: Why did towns first begin to appear, according to the passage?
options: Because rivers made it easy to travel between villages
Because farming produced enough food to support people who were not farmers
Because craftsmen needed to move away from the countryside
Because governments decided to build planned settlements
questions.explanation: The passage says towns appeared 'when farming produced enough food to feed people who did not work in the fields.'
questions.hint: Think about what needed to happen before non-farmers could settle in one place.

questions.prompt: Why were many early towns built near rivers?
options: Because rivers provided defence against enemy attack
Because rivers were the only source of building materials
Because rivers provided fresh water, a way to travel, and a means of transporting goods
Because rivers attracted wildlife that people hunted
questions.explanation: The passage lists three reasons: 'fresh water, a way to travel, and a means of transporting goods.'
questions.hint: The passage gives three specific reasons for building near rivers.

questions.prompt: What was a major public health problem in early cities?
options: Waste flowing into rivers that people drank from
Not enough workers to maintain the streets
questions.explanation: The passage explains that 'Waste from homes and workshops flowed into the same rivers that people drank from, and this contaminated water caused widespread sickness.'
questions.hint: The passage links waste disposal to a specific source of water.

questions.prompt: Despite the dangers, why did people keep moving to cities?
options: Because they were forced to by the government
Because village life had become illegal
Because cities offered new ways to earn a living and a larger community
Because cities had better farming land nearby
questions.explanation: The passage says cities offered 'the chance to earn a living in new ways and to be part of a larger community.'
questions.hint: The passage says cities offered something villages could not.

questions.prompt: Where did the Industrial Revolution begin?
questions.explanation: The passage states that the Industrial Revolution 'began in Britain in the late eighteenth century.'
questions.hint: The passage gives a clear geographic location.

questions.prompt: What replaced workshops during the Industrial Revolution?
questions.explanation: 'Factories replaced workshops, and machines did the work that had once required many skilled hands.'
questions.hint: Think about the large-scale buildings associated with industrial production.

questions.prompt: Approximately how many people lived in Manchester by 1850?
questions.explanation: The passage says Manchester had 'nearly three hundred thousand by 1850.'
questions.hint: The passage gives figures for two different years.

questions.prompt: What conditions did factory workers in industrial cities face?
options: They had comfortable housing near city parks
They lived close to factories and breathed air thick with smoke and dust
They were given free food and housing by factory owners
They worked short hours because machines did most of the work
questions.explanation: The passage says 'Workers lived close to factories, breathing air thick with smoke and dust.'
questions.hint: Consider the physical environment described near the factories.

questions.prompt: Who helped bring about improvements to city life in the nineteenth century?
options: Only factory owners who wanted healthier workers
Social reformers, journalists, and eventually governments
questions.explanation: The passage names 'Social reformers, journalists, and eventually governments' as those who responded to poor conditions.
questions.hint: The passage lists three groups who worked for change.

questions.prompt: What proportion of the world's population lives in cities today?
questions.explanation: The passage states 'Today more than half of the world's population lives in cities.'
questions.hint: The passage gives a specific current statistic.

questions.prompt: What does the passage suggest is the value of understanding how cities developed?
options: It helps governments collect more taxes
It helps us think more carefully about how to build the cities of the future
It shows that cities are always better than rural life
It proves that the Industrial Revolution was entirely beneficial
questions.explanation: The passage concludes that this understanding 'helps us think more carefully about how to build the cities of the future.'
questions.hint: The passage ends with a forward-looking statement.

cloze.template: For most of human history, people lived in small villages and relied on farming to {{b1}}. Towns only began to appear when farming produced enough food to feed people who did not work in the {{b2}}. Craftsmen, merchants, and officials could then live in one place and trade with the surrounding countryside. These early towns were often built near rivers, which provided fresh water, a way to travel, and a means of {{b3}} goods. Some towns became important trading posts and gradually attracted more people, growing into cities over many centuries. The rise of cities was not always a {{b4}} process. Disease spread easily in crowded conditions, and many early cities lost large parts of their population to plague and other illnesses. Poor {{b5}} was a constant problem. Waste from homes and workshops flowed into the same rivers that people drank from, and this contaminated water caused widespread sickness. Despite these dangers, cities continued to attract {{b6}} because they offered something that villages could not: the chance to earn a living in new ways and to be part of a larger community. The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late eighteenth century, transformed cities on a scale that had never been seen before. Factories replaced workshops, and machines did the work that had once required many {{b7}} hands. Thousands of people moved from the countryside to cities in search of factory jobs, and the populations of industrial cities grew with remarkable speed. Manchester, for example, had around twenty-five thousand {{b8}} in 1772 but nearly three hundred thousand by 1850. This rapid growth created serious problems. Housing was often overcrowded and poorly built. Workers lived close to factories, breathing air thick with smoke and {{b9}}. Children worked long hours in dangerous conditions. Social reformers, journalists, and eventually governments began to respond, passing laws that {{b10}} working hours, improved housing standards, and required clean water supplies.

blanks.explanation: 'Relied on farming to survive' is the exact phrase — 'survive' means to stay alive, the most basic motivation for farming.
The passage says 'people who did not work in the fields' — 'fields' refers to agricultural land where crops are grown.
The passage uses 'a means of transporting goods' — 'transporting' is the standard collocation with 'goods' in this context.
'Not always a smooth process' is the phrase used — 'smooth' means without difficulties or disruption.
'Poor sanitation was a constant problem' — 'sanitation' refers to systems that remove waste and keep the environment clean.
'Cities continued to attract migrants' — 'migrants' are people who move from one place to another, especially from rural to urban areas.
The passage says 'work that had once required many skilled hands' — 'skilled' describes workers with special training or expertise.
'Twenty-five thousand inhabitants' is the phrase used — 'inhabitants' is a formal word for people who live in a place.
'Air thick with smoke and dust' is the phrase — 'dust' refers to fine particles in the air from industrial processes.
'Passing laws that regulated working hours' — 'regulated' means controlled or supervised by official rules.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The passage describes both the problems and the benefits that cities have brought throughout history. Do you think the growth of cities has been mostly positive or mostly negative for human society? What evidence from the passage supports your view?
